title: Singly Linked List
description: Wrapping the raw Node chain in a proper class: insertAtEnd(), traversal, and deletion's classic 'skip over it' trick.
objectives: Wrap a linked list's raw node manipulation in a class
Implement insertAtEnd() and a print traversal
Implement deletion using the 'skip over it' pointer trick
Know the complexity of each operation

sections.text: The Linked List topic built exactly this: nodes linked in a single direction, each pointing only to next. That is a singly linked list. Now let's wrap the raw pointer manipulation in a proper class.

Recall Classes and Objects and Constructors: head is a private member, initialized to nullptr — an empty list. Keeping the raw Node manipulation inside the class is Encapsulation in action: outside code never touches next pointers directly.

Deleting a middle node never requires shifting anything (unlike an array from Arrays) — current->next = current->next->next; simply re-points around the node being removed, and delete temp; frees its heap memory. This is exactly why list-based structures are cheap to modify in the middle.

rows: Must walk to the last node first (without a tail pointer)
Must search for the node first
No indexing — must walk from head

sections.title: Removing a scavenger hunt box without disturbing the others
sections.text: Recall the scavenger hunt boxes. Removing one box from the middle of the hunt just means rewriting the note in the box BEFORE it to point straight to the box AFTER it — the removed box's own note becomes irrelevant, and no other box needs to move or be touched at all.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Forgetting to handle deleting the head node as a special case — it has no 'previous' node to rewire.
Forgetting delete temp; after unlinking a node — the memory would leak, exactly the danger covered in delete.
Losing the reference to the node being deleted before unlinking it — save it in a temp variable first, then rewire, then delete.
Assuming insertAtEnd() is O(1) — without a separately tracked tail pointer, it requires walking the entire list first.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: Why does a singly linked list only need to store a pointer to its head?
questions.answer: Because every other node is reachable by following next pointers starting from the head — no other reference is needed to access the whole list.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of inserting at the end of a singly linked list without a tail pointer?
questions.answer: O(n) — the list must be traversed from the head to find the last node before linking the new one.

questions.question: How do you delete a node from the middle of a singly linked list?
questions.answer: Find the node just before the one to delete, then re-point its next to skip over the target node (current->next = current->next->next;), and free the removed node's memory with delete.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: A singly linked list wraps raw Node/next manipulation in a class holding just a head pointer, from which the entire list is reachable. insertAtEnd() walks to the last node (O(n) without a tracked tail) and links a new one; deletion finds the node just before the target and re-points around it with current->next = current->next->next;, then frees the removed node with delete — no shifting required, unlike an array. Insert at head is O(1), but insert at end, delete by value, and search are all O(n), since there's no indexed access — only sequential traversal from head.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Singly Linked List
sections.text: head = the only thing you need to remember; everything else follows next. Delete = rewrite the note before it to skip straight past, then free the removed box.
LeetCode: Singly Linked List in Practice

sections.title: Reverse Linked List
sections.problemStatement: Given the head of a singly linked list, reverse it and return the new head.
hints: Every node's next pointer needs to flip direction — walking forward while rewiring backward needs three tracking pointers: previous, current, and next (saved before it's overwritten).
At each step: save current->next, then point current->next backward at prev, then advance prev and current forward.
The new head is whatever node was current when the loop ends (the old list's last node).

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Copy every value into a vector, then overwrite the list's node values in reverse order — reverses the data without touching any pointers.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Iterative three-pointer reversal — rewires the existing nodes' next pointers directly, in a single O(1)-space pass.

sections.title: Middle of the Linked List
sections.problemStatement: Given the head of a singly linked list, return the middle node. If there are two middle nodes, return the second one.
hints: A linked list has no length property or indexed access — finding 'the middle' requires either counting first, or a cleverer single-pass trick.
The slow/fast pointer technique: one pointer moves one step at a time, another moves two steps at a time. When the fast pointer reaches the end, the slow pointer is exactly at the middle.
This works because the fast pointer covers twice the distance in the same number of steps — by the time it's done the whole list, slow has only covered half.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Count the list's length first, then walk again to the middle index.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Slow/fast pointers: slow moves one step, fast moves two — a single pass finds the middle.

sections.title: Linked List Cycle
sections.problemStatement: Given the head of a linked list, return true if the list has a cycle (some node's next eventually points back to an earlier node).

examples.input: head = [3,2,0,-4], tail connects to node index 1

hints: A cycle means there's no true 'end' (nullptr) to reach — walking forward forever would never terminate naturally.
The same slow/fast pointer idea from Middle of the Linked List detects this: if there's a cycle, the faster pointer will eventually LAP the slower one and they'll meet at the same node.
If the fast pointer ever reaches nullptr, there's no cycle — the list has a genuine end.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Track every visited node's address in a hash set — if a node is ever visited twice, there's a cycle.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Floyd's cycle detection: slow and fast pointers, meeting only if a cycle exists — no extra memory needed.

sections.title: Merge Two Sorted Lists
sections.problemStatement: Given the heads of two sorted linked lists, merge them into one sorted list and return its head.
hints: A dummy 'placeholder' head node before the real list simplifies building a new list — no special-casing 'is this the first node' needed.
Walk both lists together with a tail pointer, always attaching the smaller of the two current nodes and advancing that list.
Once one list runs out, attach whatever remains of the other directly — it's already sorted, no more comparisons needed.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Collect all values, sort them, then build a brand new list from scratch.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n+m) for new nodes and values

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Iterative merge using a dummy head and a tail pointer, reusing the existing nodes — no sorting, no new list built from scratch.

sections.title: Remove Duplicates from Sorted List
sections.problemStatement: Given the head of a sorted linked list, delete all duplicates so each element appears once, and return the list.
hints: Since the list is SORTED, duplicates of a value are always immediately adjacent — no need to look far to spot one.
This is the deletion 'skip over it' trick from this lesson's Section 5, applied repeatedly: while the current node's next has the same value, skip it.
Only advance the current pointer once its next node has a genuinely different value.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Collect unique values using a hash set, then rebuild the list from scratch with only those values.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) for the seen set and new nodes

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Single pass, skip-over-it deletion — exploits sortedness so adjacent duplicates can be removed directly, in place.

sections.title: Remove Linked List Elements
sections.problemStatement: Given the head of a linked list and an integer val, remove all nodes with that value and return the new head.
hints: Deleting the HEAD node is a special case (no 'previous' node exists) — this lesson's deleteValue() has to check for it separately.
A dummy node placed BEFORE the real head sidesteps that special case entirely: now every node to delete, including the original head, has a 'previous' node.
Walk with a pointer that always looks one node ahead — skip over (delete) any node whose value matches, otherwise advance.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: This lesson's own deleteValue() logic, called repeatedly until no more matches exist — handling the head specially each time.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: A dummy node before head eliminates the special-case head-deletion branch entirely — one uniform loop handles every deletion.

sections.title: Remove Nth Node From End of List
sections.problemStatement: Given the head of a linked list, remove the n-th node from the END of the list, and return its head.
hints: Without a length property, 'nth from the end' normally requires counting the length first, then walking again — two passes.
A single-pass trick: advance a 'fast' pointer n steps ahead first, THEN move both slow and fast together — by the time fast reaches the end, slow is exactly n nodes from the end.
A dummy node before head, same as Remove Linked List Elements, handles the edge case of removing the head itself cleanly.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Count the list's length first, then walk again to the node just before the one to remove.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Two pointers with an n-node gap: advance fast n steps first, then move both together until fast reaches the end.
